question: [File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged front-runner, observing a pending mempool transaction containing a valid Authorization signature with nonce==0, submit that same signature first, consuming nonce 0 and incrementing nonce[authorizer] to 1, so that the original submitter's transaction reverts with InvalidNonce(), permanently blocking the authorizer from using that signed message and forcing them to re-sign with nonce==1? Preconditions: authorizer has called midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), true, authorizer); authorizer signs Authorization{nonce:0, deadline:future}; tx is visible in mempool. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) with the stolen sig; (2) original submitter's tx reverts. Invariant tested: a valid signed authorization must be submittable exactly once by the intended party without front-run interference. Scoped impact: gate bypass / unauthorized nonce consumption causing DoS on delegated authorization. Proof idea: fork-test with vm.prank(attacker) submitting before original sender; assert original tx reverts and nonce[authorizer]==1.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged delegate B, who has been authorized by A in Midnight (isAuthorized[A][B]==true), sign an Authorization struct with authorization.authorizer==A and authorization.authorized==C, isAuthorized==true, and submit it to EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, thereby granting C full Midnight authorization on behalf of A without A ever signing anything? Preconditions: A calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), true, A); A calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(B, true, A); B signs Authorization{authorizer:A, authorized:C, isAuthorized:true, nonce:0, deadline:future}. Call sequence: (1) B signs auth struct; (2) anyone calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig_by_B); (3) assert midnight.isAuthorized(A, C)==true. Invariant tested: only the authorizer or their explicitly intended delegate should be able to grant new authorizations; a pre-existing Midnight authorization of B by A should not transitively allow B to grant arbitrary new authorizations via EcrecoverAuthorizer. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure/withdrawal — C can now withdraw A's credit or collateral. Proof idea: unit test asserting midnight.isAuthorized(A, C)==true after B signs and submits.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged delegate B, authorized by A in Midnight, sign an Authorization{authorizer:A, authorized:B, isAuthorized:true} to grant themselves additional Midnight authorization, then use that to call midnight.setIsAuthorized(C, true, A) directly, creating an unbounded authorization chain from A to arbitrary addresses? Preconditions: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer and B in Midnight; B signs self-authorization struct. Call sequence: (1) B signs Authorization{authorizer:A, authorized:B, isAuthorized:true, nonce:0}; (2) B submits to ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; (3) B calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(C, true, A); (4) C calls midnight.withdraw on A's credit. Invariant tested: authorization must not be self-amplifying; a delegate must not be able to grant themselves or others permissions beyond what the authorizer explicitly intended. Scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal of A's funds by C. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting midnight.isAuthorized(A, C)==true and loanToken.balanceOf(C) increases.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged delegate B, authorized by A in Midnight, sign an Authorization{authorizer:A, authorized:X, isAuthorized:false} to revoke A's authorization of a critical operator X (e.g., a liquidation bot or a trusted bundler), causing A's positions to become unliquidatable or A's operations to be DoS'd? Preconditions: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer and B in Midnight; A has also authorized X; B signs revocation auth struct with nonce==current nonce[A]. Call sequence: (1) B signs Authorization{authorizer:A, authorized:X, isAuthorized:false, nonce:N}; (2) B submits to ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; (3) assert midnight.isAuthorized(A, X)==false. Invariant tested: only the authorizer should be able to revoke their own delegations; a delegate must not be able to revoke other delegations. Scoped impact: fund freeze — A's authorized operator X can no longer act on A's behalf. Proof idea: unit test asserting midnight.isAuthorized(A, X)==false after B's submission.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a nonce desync by submitting a valid Authorization with nonce==N, then immediately submitting another valid Authorization with nonce==N+1 (both signed by the authorizer), so that the authorizer's intended sequential revocation (nonce N+1 revokes what nonce N granted) is executed in the wrong order if the attacker reorders them? Preconditions: authorizer signs two authorizations: auth0{nonce:0, isAuthorized:true} and auth1{nonce:1, isAuthorized:false}; both are in mempool. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits auth1 first (reverts with InvalidNonce since nonce==0 expected); (2) attacker submits auth0; (3) auth1 is now stale. Invariant tested: sequential nonce must enforce ordering; out-of-order submission must revert. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — authorization state may not reflect authorizer's intent if ordering is violated. Proof idea: fuzz test submitting auth structs in random order and assert only the correct sequential order succeeds.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the inclusive deadline check (block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) to submit an Authorization at exactly block.timestamp == deadline, which is the last valid block, potentially in the same block as a revocation transaction, causing a race condition where the authorization is granted and immediately used before the revocation lands? Preconditions: authorizer signs Authorization{deadline: T}; at block T, authorizer also submits a direct midnight.setIsAuthorized revocation; both txs are in the same block. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized at block.timestamp==T; (2) authorization is granted; (3) attacker immediately calls midnight.withdraw on behalf of authorizer; (4) revocation tx lands after. Invariant tested: deadline==block.timestamp must be treated as expired or valid consistently; the inclusive boundary must not create a window for exploitation. Scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal using a just-expired authorization. Proof idea: unit test with vm.warp(deadline) asserting setIsAuthorized succeeds and subsequent action succeeds.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization with deadline==type(uint256).max, which never expires, creating a permanent delegation that the authorizer cannot revoke without incrementing their nonce (which requires signing a new message), effectively locking in an authorization indefinitely if the authorizer loses access to their signing key? Preconditions: authorizer signs Authorization{deadline: type(uint256).max, isAuthorized:true}; signature is leaked or front-run. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits auth with max deadline; (2) authorization is permanent until nonce is incremented by a new signed message. Invariant tested: authorizations must be revocable by the authorizer at any time without requiring a new signature; a max-deadline authorization must not create an irrevocable state. Scoped impact: fund freeze / unauthorized seizure — authorized party retains access indefinitely. Proof idea: unit test asserting that after max-deadline auth is submitted, the only revocation path requires a new signed message with nonce+1.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH] Can an unprivileged attacker replay an Authorization signature signed for EcrecoverAuthorizer on a different deployed instance of EcrecoverAuthorizer (e.g., a second deployment at a different address), given that the domain separator includes address(this) but the authorizer may not realize there are multiple deployments? Preconditions: two EcrecoverAuthorizer instances deployed at addr1 and addr2; authorizer signs for addr1; attacker submits to addr2 where nonce[authorizer]==0. Call sequence: (1) authorizer signs Authorization for addr1 with nonce==0; (2) attacker submits same sig to addr2.setIsAuthorized; (3) ecrecover produces different digest (different verifyingContract), so sig is invalid. Invariant tested: domain separator must bind signatures to a specific verifyingContract; cross-instance replay must be impossible. Scoped impact: gate bypass if domain binding is insufficient. Proof idea: differential test computing domain separators for two addresses and asserting they differ.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the non-standard EIP712Domain type (missing 'name' and 'version' fields: keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)')) to cause a wallet or signing library to produce a signature that is valid on-chain but was displayed to the user under a different domain context, enabling a social-engineering or UI-spoofing attack where the user believes they are signing for a different protocol? Preconditions: user uses a wallet that displays EIP-712 domain fields; the missing name/version fields cause the wallet to show incomplete domain info. Call sequence: (1) attacker presents a signing request with a crafted domain; (2) user signs believing it is for a different contract; (3) attacker submits to EcrecoverAuthorizer. Invariant tested: the EIP-712 domain must uniquely and unambiguously identify the signing context to prevent cross-protocol signature confusion. Scoped impact: gate bypass — user unknowingly grants authorization. Proof idea: differential test comparing AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH against keccak256 of the full type string and asserting the domain is non-standard.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker, on a chain that has forked from the original chain but where the EcrecoverAuthorizer was deployed at the same address (e.g., via CREATE2 or deterministic deployment), replay an Authorization signature from the original chain on the forked chain, since the domain separator uses block.chainid computed at call time and the forked chain has a different chainid? Preconditions: original chain has chainid=1; forked chain has chainid=2; EcrecoverAuthorizer deployed at same address on both; authorizer signed for chainid=1. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits original chain sig to forked chain EcrecoverAuthorizer; (2) ecrecover computes digest with chainid=2, producing different digest; (3) sig is invalid. Invariant tested: domain separator must bind to block.chainid at call time; cross-chain replay must be impossible. Scoped impact: gate bypass on forked chain. Proof idea: unit test with vm.chainId(2) asserting the sig from chainid=1 is rejected.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized is permissionless (anyone can submit a valid sig) to front-run a revocation: the authorizer signs auth{nonce:N, isAuthorized:false} to revoke B, but the attacker front-runs with auth{nonce:N, isAuthorized:true} (a previously signed but unsubmitted grant), consuming nonce N and preventing the revocation from landing? Preconditions: authorizer previously signed auth{nonce:N, isAuthorized:true} but never submitted it; authorizer now signs auth{nonce:N, isAuthorized:false} to revoke; attacker has the old signed grant. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits old grant with nonce N; (2) nonce becomes N+1; (3) authorizer's revocation with nonce N reverts with InvalidNonce. Invariant tested: a revocation must be submittable without being front-run by a stale grant with the same nonce. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure — B retains authorization after authorizer intended to revoke. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting midnight.isAuthorized(A, B)==true after front-run and revocation fails.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a nonce desync between EcrecoverAuthorizer.nonce[authorizer] and the authorizer's expected nonce by submitting a valid Authorization with nonce==0 (the first ever), so that all pre-signed authorizations with nonce==0 that the authorizer intended to submit later are now invalid, forcing the authorizer to re-sign everything? Preconditions: authorizer has pre-signed multiple Authorization structs all with nonce==0 (e.g., for different authorized addresses); attacker observes one and submits it. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits any one of the nonce==0 auths; (2) nonce[authorizer] becomes 1; (3) all other nonce==0 auths are now invalid. Invariant tested: sequential nonce must not allow a single submission to invalidate multiple pre-signed authorizations. Scoped impact: fund freeze — authorizer cannot use pre-signed authorizations. Proof idea: fuzz test with multiple pre-signed nonce==0 auths asserting only one can succeed.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.multicall] Can an unprivileged attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically call setIsAuthorized (granting themselves authorization via a stolen sig) and then immediately call withdraw/withdrawCollateral on behalf of the victim, all in a single transaction before any revocation can land? Preconditions: attacker has a valid Authorization sig granting themselves authorization; victim has credit or collateral in Midnight. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls midnight.multicall([ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig), midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker)]); but note multicall only calls midnight functions, not external contracts. Invariant tested: multicall must not allow atomic authorization-grant-and-exploit in a single tx. Scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal. Proof idea: unit test attempting multicall with setIsAuthorized followed by withdraw and asserting it fails or succeeds as expected.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified + CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged attacker craft a ratifierData payload that causes EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified to return CALLBACK_SUCCESS (keccak256('morpho.midnight.callbackSuccess')) without a valid signature, by exploiting the fact that CALLBACK_SUCCESS is a well-known constant and the ratifier is a view function that returns it on success? Preconditions: attacker creates an offer with offer.ratifier pointing to a malicious contract that always returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS; market has no gate. Call sequence: (1) attacker deploys MaliciousRatifier that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS unconditionally; (2) attacker creates offer with ratifier=MaliciousRatifier; (3) attacker calls midnight.take with the offer; (4) isRatified returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Invariant tested: CALLBACK_SUCCESS must only be returnable by a ratifier that has genuinely verified the offer; the constant must not be guessable or trivially returnable. Scoped impact: gate bypass — offer is filled without maker's consent. Proof idea: unit test with DummyRatifier (which already does this) asserting that any ratifier can return CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker replay a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature (root + proof + sig) across multiple take calls for the same offer, since EcrecoverRatifier has no per-fill nonce and only checks isRootCanceled, allowing the same signed root to authorize unlimited fills until the maker cancels? Preconditions: maker signs a root containing a single offer with maxUnits=1000; taker fills 100 units; taker fills another 100 units with the same ratifierData. Call sequence: (1) taker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, 100, ...); (2) taker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, 100, ...) again; (3) assert consumed[maker][group] increases each time. Invariant tested: a single ratifier signature must not authorize more fills than the maker intended; the consumed tracking in Midnight must be the only fill cap. Scoped impact: offer overfill — maker's offer is filled beyond intent if consumed tracking is insufficient. Proof idea: fuzz test with repeated take calls using same ratifierData asserting consumed[maker][group] is bounded by offer.maxUnits.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged delegate B, authorized by maker A in Midnight, call EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(A, root) to cancel A's signed offer tree root, effectively DoS-ing all of A's pending offers that depend on that root, without A's consent? Preconditions: A authorizes B in Midnight (isAuthorized[A][B]==true); A has signed a root covering multiple offers; B calls cancelRoot(A, root). Call sequence: (1) B calls ecrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(A, root); (2) isRootCanceled[A][root] becomes true; (3) any taker calling midnight.take with A's offer and that root gets RootCanceled revert. Invariant tested: root cancellation must only be callable by the maker or their explicitly intended delegate for that purpose; a general Midnight authorization must not grant root cancellation rights. Scoped impact: fund freeze / offer DoS — A's offers become unfillable. Proof idea: unit test asserting isRootCanceled[A][root]==true after B's call and subsequent take reverts.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified checks isAuthorized at call time (not at signing time) to use a signature from a signer whose authorization was revoked after signing but before the take call, causing the ratifier to reject a legitimately signed offer? Preconditions: maker A authorizes signer B; B signs a root; A revokes B's authorization before the take; taker submits take with B's sig. Call sequence: (1) A authorizes B; (2) B signs root; (3) A revokes B (midnight.setIsAuthorized(B, false, A)); (4) taker calls midnight.take with B's sig; (5) isRatified reverts with Unauthorized. Invariant tested: ratifier authorization check at call time must not create a DoS vector where a maker can grief their own offers by revoking a signer. Scoped impact: fund freeze — valid offers become unfillable after signer revocation. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting take reverts after signer revocation.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified + HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by submitting a ratifierData with proof.length > 20, causing HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash to revert with TreeTooHigh(), making the take call revert and blocking the taker from filling a valid offer? Preconditions: taker calls midnight.take with ratifierData containing a proof array of length 21; the offer is otherwise valid. Call sequence: (1) taker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData_with_proof_length_21, units, ...); (2) isRatified calls HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash(21); (3) reverts with TreeTooHigh. Invariant tested: a taker must not be able to DoS a take by providing an oversized proof; the ratifier must validate proof length before reverting. Scoped impact: low-cost DoS — taker cannot fill offer with oversized proof. Proof idea: fuzz test with proof.length in [0, 25] asserting revert for length > 20.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer calls IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).setIsAuthorized(authorization.authorized, authorization.isAuthorized, authorization.authorizer) — where msg.sender is ecrecoverAuthorizer — to succeed only if midnight.isAuthorized[authorization.authorizer][ecrecoverAuthorizer]==true, and if the authorizer has not pre-authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight, the call reverts, causing a silent failure mode where a valid signature cannot be submitted? Preconditions: authorizer signs a valid Authorization but has NOT called midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), true, authorizer). Call sequence: (1) anyone calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig); (2) nonce is incremented; (3) IMidnight.setIsAuthorized reverts with Unauthorized; (4) entire tx reverts; (5) nonce increment is also reverted. Invariant tested: nonce must not be permanently consumed if the downstream Midnight call reverts; the nonce increment must be atomic with the Midnight state change. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — nonce desync if nonce is consumed without Midnight state change. Proof idea: unit test asserting nonce[authorizer] is unchanged after a failed setIsAuthorized call.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a nonce desync by first getting the authorizer to pre-authorize ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight, then having the authorizer revoke ecrecoverAuthorizer's Midnight authorization, then submitting a valid Authorization sig: the nonce check passes and nonce is incremented, but the IMidnight.setIsAuthorized call reverts, causing the entire tx to revert and the nonce to be un-incremented, leaving the nonce unchanged but the sig consumed (since the tx reverted)? Preconditions: authorizer pre-authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer; authorizer signs auth{nonce:0}; authorizer revokes ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight; attacker submits auth. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig); (2) nonce check passes, nonce incremented to 1; (3) IMidnight.setIsAuthorized reverts; (4) entire tx reverts, nonce back to 0; (5) sig with nonce==0 can be resubmitted after re-authorization. Invariant tested: a reverted submission must not permanently consume the nonce; the sig must remain replayable after the revert. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — sig can be replayed after re-authorization. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting nonce[authorizer]==0 after failed submission and sig is replayable.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH] Can an unprivileged attacker verify that the hardcoded AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH (0x81d0284f...) matches keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)') and that any deviation (e.g., field reordering, type change) would cause all signatures to be invalid, creating a deployment-time vulnerability if the typehash constant is wrong? Preconditions: AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH is a hardcoded constant; the type string is defined in a comment. Call sequence: (1) compute keccak256(AUTHORIZATION_TYPE) off-chain; (2) compare to AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH; (3) assert equality. Invariant tested: AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH must exactly match the keccak256 of the canonical type string; any mismatch causes all signatures to be permanently invalid. Scoped impact: fund freeze — all EcrecoverAuthorizer-based authorizations fail if typehash is wrong. Proof idea: unit test asserting AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH == keccak256(AUTHORIZATION_TYPE) as in SetIsAuthorizedWithSigTest.testAuthorizationTypeHash.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified + HashLib.hashOffer] Can an unprivileged attacker craft two different Offer structs that produce the same hashOffer output (a collision in the EIP-712 offer hash), allowing a signature for one offer to be used to ratify a different offer with different terms (e.g., different maxUnits, tick, or market)? Preconditions: attacker finds two Offer structs O1 and O2 where HashLib.hashOffer(O1) == HashLib.hashOffer(O2); maker signs a root containing O1; attacker submits take with O2 and the same ratifierData. Call sequence: (1) attacker finds collision; (2) maker signs root for O1; (3) attacker calls midnight.take(O2, ratifierData_for_O1, ...); (4) HashLib.isLeaf passes because hashOffer(O2)==hashOffer(O1). Invariant tested: hashOffer must be collision-resistant; no two distinct offers must produce the same hash. Scoped impact: offer bug — maker's signature is used to fill an offer with different terms. Proof idea: differential fuzz test generating random Offer pairs and asserting hashOffer(O1) != hashOffer(O2) when O1 != O2.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified + HashLib.isLeaf] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the leafIndex-based Merkle proof verification in HashLib.isLeaf to submit a valid proof for a different leaf position (different leafIndex) than the one the maker intended, allowing a different offer in the tree to be filled using a proof intended for another leaf? Preconditions: maker signs a root containing offers [O0, O1]; attacker has proof for O0 (leafIndex=0); attacker submits take for O1 with leafIndex=0 and proof for O0. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls midnight.take(O1, ratifierData{sig, root, leafIndex:0, proof_for_O0}, ...); (2) HashLib.isLeaf(root, hashOffer(O1), 0, proof_for_O0) returns false; (3) reverts with InvalidProof. Invariant tested: isLeaf must bind the leaf hash to the correct position; a proof for one leaf must not validate a different leaf. Scoped impact: offer bug — wrong offer filled using mismatched proof. Proof idea: unit test asserting isLeaf returns false when leafIndex and proof are mismatched.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that ecrecover returns address(0) for invalid signatures, and the check require(signer != address(0), InvalidSignature()) prevents address(0) from being treated as a valid signer, but if authorization.authorizer is address(0) and the check order is wrong, could address(0) be authorized? Preconditions: attacker crafts Authorization{authorizer: address(0), ...}; submits with any signature that causes ecrecover to return address(0). Call sequence: (1) attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth{authorizer:address(0)}, malformed_sig); (2) ecrecover returns address(0); (3) require(signer != address(0)) reverts with InvalidSignature. Invariant tested: address(0) must never be treated as a valid signer or authorizer; the zero-address check must precede the authorizer equality check. Scoped impact: gate bypass if address(0) could be authorized. Proof idea: unit test asserting setIsAuthorized reverts with InvalidSignature when ecrecover returns address(0).

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit signature malleability (using the complementary (v, r, -s mod n) signature) to submit a second valid submission of the same Authorization, bypassing the nonce check since the nonce was already incremented by the first submission? Preconditions: authorizer signs Authorization{nonce:0}; first submission succeeds and nonce becomes 1; attacker computes malleable sig (v', r, s') from original sig; attacker tries to submit malleable sig with nonce==0. Call sequence: (1) original sig submitted, nonce becomes 1; (2) attacker submits malleable sig with nonce==0; (3) nonce check fails (expected 1, got 0); (4) reverts with InvalidNonce. Invariant tested: sequential nonce must prevent replay of malleable signatures; nonce increment must make all prior nonce values invalid. Scoped impact: accounting corruption — if nonce check is bypassed, authorization state could be replayed. Proof idea: unit test computing malleable sig and asserting it reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged front-runner, observing a pending mempool transaction containing a valid Authorization signature with nonce==0, submit that same signature first, consuming nonce 0 and incrementing nonce[authorizer] to 1, so that the original submitter's transaction reverts with InvalidNonce(), permanently blocking the authorizer from using that signed message and forcing them to re-sign with nonce==1?
